findings.title: SQL Injection — UNION + Boolean-based on id parameter
findings.description: The id parameter on /vulnerabilities/sqli/ is injectable, allowing attackers to extract database contents, enumerate users, and escalate to full DB compromise.
findings.evidence: sqlmap boolean, union, and time-based payloads confirmed; extracted user/password hashes from dvwa.users.

findings.title: OS Command Injection — ip parameter shell-out
findings.description: The ip parameter on /vulnerabilities/exec/ is unsafely passed to the shell, allowing remote command execution as www-data.

findings.title: Unrestricted PHP File Upload
findings.description: The /vulnerabilities/upload/ endpoint allows uploading of arbitrary PHP files, resulting in persistent remote code execution.
findings.evidence: shell.php uploaded and executed; response shows uid=33(www-data).

findings.title: Stored Cross-Site Scripting — guestbook
findings.description: User input in the mtxMessage parameter on /vulnerabilities/xss_s/ is stored and rendered without encoding, allowing persistent XSS.
findings.evidence: Stored <script> payload executes on page load for all users.

findings.title: Reflected Cross-Site Scripting — name parameter
findings.description: The name parameter on /vulnerabilities/xss_r/ is reflected unsanitized, allowing attackers to inject and execute JavaScript in victims' browsers.
findings.evidence: GET .../xss_r/?name= reflected in HTML.

findings.title: Local File Inclusion — page parameter
findings.description: The page parameter on /vulnerabilities/fi/ is used in a file include without validation, allowing attackers to read arbitrary files from the server.

findings.title: Cross-Site Request Forgery — password change
findings.description: The /vulnerabilities/csrf/ endpoint allows password changes via GET without CSRF protection, enabling attackers to force password changes for authenticated users.
findings.evidence: No anti-CSRF token; PoC HTML auto-submits password change.

findings.title: Weak/Default Credentials — admin:password, others
findings.description: Multiple default or weak credential pairs (e.g., admin:password) are accepted on /login.php, allowing trivial account takeover.
findings.evidence: admin:password, gordonb:abc123, etc. all accepted; session cookie set.

findings.title: Directory Listing — /config/ browseable
findings.description: The /config/ directory is publicly browseable, exposing sensitive files such as config.inc.php.dist containing DB credentials.
findings.evidence: GET /config/ lists config.inc.php.dist with DB creds.

findings.title: phpinfo() Exposed — /phpinfo.php
findings.description: The /phpinfo.php endpoint is accessible and discloses full PHP environment, loaded modules, and dangerous function availability.
findings.evidence: GET /phpinfo.php returns PHP 7.4.33, loaded extensions, open_basedir: no value.

findings.title: TLS 1.0/1.1 Enabled + Weak CBC Ciphers
findings.description: The server offers deprecated TLS 1.0/1.1 and weak CBC cipher suites, reducing transport security.
findings.evidence: TLS 1.0/1.1 offered; weak CBC suites enabled.

findings.title: Missing Content-Security-Policy Header
findings.description: No Content-Security-Policy header is set on any major endpoint, increasing XSS risk.

findings.title: Cookie Missing Secure and HttpOnly Flags
findings.description: Session cookies (PHPSESSID, security) are set without Secure or HttpOnly flags, exposing them to theft via XSS or network.
findings.evidence: Set-Cookie: PHPSESSID=...; no Secure; no HttpOnly.

findings.title: Missing X-Frame-Options / frame-ancestors
findings.description: No X-Frame-Options or frame-ancestors CSP is set, allowing the site to be framed and increasing clickjacking risk.

findings.title: robots.txt Enumerates Sensitive Paths
findings.description: The robots.txt file lists /vulnerabilities/, /config/, and /docs/, aiding attackers in discovering sensitive areas.

findings.title: Server Banner Discloses Apache Version
findings.description: The Server header reveals Apache/2.4.52 (Ubuntu), aiding attackers in targeting known CVEs.

findings.title: Backend Technology Disclosed — PHP/7.4.33 (EOL)
findings.description: The X-Powered-By header and phpinfo.php reveal PHP 7.4.33, which is end-of-life and vulnerable.

test_cases.title: [SETUP] Authentication bootstrap — run this FIRST
test_cases.steps: ["$base = \"https://pentest-ground.com:4280\"", "$user = \"admin\"; $pass = \"password\"  # resolve kv/dvwa/admin", "$page = Invoke-WebRequest \"$base/login.php\" -SessionVariable s -UseBasicParsing", "$tok = [regex]::Match($page.Content, \"user_token'\\s*value='([a-f0-9]+)\").Groups[1].Value", "$null = Invoke-WebRequest \"$base/login.php\" -Method POST -WebSession $s -UseBasicParsing -Body @{ username = $user; password = $pass; Login = 'Login'; user_token = $tok }", "$env:PHPSESSID = ($s.Cookies.GetCookies($base) | Where-Object Name -eq 'PHPSESSID').Value", "\"PHPSESSID=$($env:PHPSESSID)\"   # confirm a value was minted"]
test_cases.expected_result: Prints a non-empty PHPSESSID=… value. Every auth-required test case below reuses $env:PHPSESSID (and the security cookie), so run this once per session before copying those curls. If the value is blank, the login failed — check the credentials.
test_cases.curl_command:
$base = "https://pentest-ground.com:4280"
$user = "admin"; $pass = "password"  # resolve kv/dvwa/admin
$page = Invoke-WebRequest "$base/login.php" -SessionVariable s -UseBasicParsing
$tok = [regex]::Match($page.Content, "user_token'\s*value='([a-f0-9]+)").Groups[1].Value
$null = Invoke-WebRequest "$base/login.php" -Method POST -WebSession $s -UseBasicParsing -Body @{ username = $user; password = $pass; Login = 'Login'; user_token = $tok }
$env:PHPSESSID = ($s.Cookies.GetCookies($base) | Where-Object Name -eq 'PHPSESSID').Value
"PHPSESSID=$($env:PHPSESSID)"   # confirm a value was minted

test_cases.source_finding: Business Logic Bypass — Negative Amounts and Invalid Data Accepted
test_cases.title: [A06] Business Logic Bypass — Negative Amounts and Invalid Data Accepted — Submit logically invalid data (e.g., negative/zero values) in guestbook message
test_cases.steps: ["Log in using valid credentials (e.g., admin:password).", "Send a POST request to /vulnerabilities/xss_s/ with mtxMessage set to -100.", "Check if the guestbook displays or stores the negative value."]
test_cases.expected_result: Vulnerable: The guestbook accepts and displays the negative value (-100) without validation or error.
test_cases.http_request:
POST /vulnerabilities/xss_s/ HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Cookie: PHPSESSID=<valid-session>

txtName=TestUser&mtxMessage=-100&btnSign=Sign+Guestbook
test_cases.patched_response: Patched: The application rejects the input and returns an error message or validation warning.
test_cases.remediation: Implement plausibility checks and input validation at all tiers to reject logically invalid data as per OWASP prevention guidance.

test_cases.source_finding: Missing Rate Limiting on Login — Brute Force Protection Absent
test_cases.title: [A06] Missing Rate Limiting on Login — Brute Force Protection Absent — Test login endpoint for rate limiting by sending multiple rapid login attempts
test_cases.steps: ["Log in to obtain a valid session cookie.", "Configure Burp Intruder to send 100 rapid GET requests to /vulnerabilities/brute/ with varying passwords.", "Observe if the application blocks or throttles requests after several failed attempts."]
test_cases.expected_result: Vulnerable: All requests are processed without delay or blocking; no lockout or throttling occurs.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /vulnerabilities/brute/?username=admin&password=wrongpass&Login=Login HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Cookie: PHPSESSID=<valid-session>
test_cases.patched_response: Patched: The application enforces rate limiting or account lockout after several failed attempts.
test_cases.remediation: Implement rate limiting and lockout mechanisms for authentication endpoints as per OWASP secure design guidance.

test_cases.source_finding: Predictable Resource IDs — Sequential or Timestamp-Based IDs
test_cases.title: [A06] Predictable Resource IDs — Sequential or Timestamp-Based IDs — Test for predictable ID values in SQLi endpoint
test_cases.steps: ["Log in to obtain a valid session.", "Send GET requests to /vulnerabilities/sqli/ with id values incremented sequentially (e.g., 1, 2, 3, ...).", "Check if each ID returns valid user data, indicating predictability."]
test_cases.expected_result: Vulnerable: Each sequential ID returns valid data, confirming IDs are predictable and not randomized.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /vulnerabilities/sqli/?id=1&Submit=Submit HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Cookie: PHPSESSID=<valid-session>
test_cases.patched_response: Patched: IDs are non-sequential, randomized, or access is denied for unauthorized IDs.
test_cases.remediation: Use non-predictable, random resource identifiers and enforce access controls as per OWASP secure design patterns.

test_cases.source_finding: Directory Listing Enabled — Sensitive Files Exposed
test_cases.title: [A06] Directory Listing Enabled — Sensitive Files Exposed — Browse /config/ directory for sensitive files
test_cases.steps: ["Send a GET request to /config/ without authentication.", "Check if the directory listing is displayed and sensitive files (e.g., config.inc.php.dist) are accessible.", "Attempt to download exposed files."]
test_cases.expected_result: Vulnerable: Directory listing is enabled and sensitive files are downloadable.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /config/ HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
test_cases.patched_response: Patched: Directory listing is disabled; direct access returns 403 or blank page.
test_cases.remediation: Disable directory listing and restrict access to sensitive files as per OWASP secure deployment guidance.

test_cases.source_finding: Password Change via GET — Weak Workflow Design
test_cases.title: [A06] Password Change via GET — Weak Workflow Design — Change password using GET request without CSRF protection or old password
test_cases.steps: ["Log in as a valid user.", "Send a GET request to /vulnerabilities/csrf/ with password_new and password_conf parameters.", "Verify if password is changed without requiring old password or CSRF token."]
test_cases.expected_result: Vulnerable: Password is changed via GET request without CSRF token or old password validation.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /vulnerabilities/csrf/?password_new=NewPass123!&password_conf=NewPass123!&Change=Change HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Cookie: PHPSESSID=<valid-session>
test_cases.patched_response: Patched: Password change requires POST, CSRF token, and old password validation.
test_cases.remediation: Redesign password change workflow to require POST, CSRF token, and old password as per OWASP secure design guidance.

test_cases.source_finding: Forced Browsing — Accessing Authenticated Pages Without Login
test_cases.title: [A06] Forced Browsing — Accessing Authenticated Pages Without Login — Attempt to access /index.php without authentication
test_cases.steps: ["Send a GET request to /index.php without any session cookie.", "Check if the page loads or redirects to login.", "Verify if any sensitive data is exposed."]
test_cases.expected_result: Vulnerable: Page loads and exposes authenticated content without login.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /index.php HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
test_cases.patched_response: Patched: Redirects to login page or returns 401/403 error.
test_cases.remediation: Enforce authentication checks on all sensitive endpoints as per OWASP secure design lifecycle.

test_cases.source_finding: Sensitive Information Disclosure via robots.txt — Information Leakage
test_cases.title: [A06] Sensitive Information Disclosure via robots.txt — Information Leakage — Review robots.txt for sensitive paths
test_cases.steps: ["Send a GET request to /robots.txt.", "Check for Disallow entries referencing sensitive directories (e.g., /vulnerabilities/, /config/, /docs/).", "Verify if these paths are accessible."]
test_cases.expected_result: Vulnerable: robots.txt reveals sensitive paths aiding attackers in recon.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /robots.txt HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
test_cases.patched_response: Patched: robots.txt does not reference sensitive directories or is absent.
test_cases.remediation: Remove sensitive paths from robots.txt and restrict access to those directories as per OWASP secure design guidance.

test_cases.source_finding: Missing Security Headers — CSP, X-Frame-Options Absent
test_cases.title: [A06] Missing Security Headers — CSP, X-Frame-Options Absent — Check for missing Content-Security-Policy and X-Frame-Options headers
test_cases.steps: ["Send a GET request to /login.php.", "Review response headers for Content-Security-Policy and X-Frame-Options.", "Verify if headers are missing."]
test_cases.expected_result: Vulnerable: No CSP or X-Frame-Options headers present in response.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /login.php HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
test_cases.patched_response: Patched: Response includes CSP and X-Frame-Options headers.
test_cases.remediation: Add Content-Security-Policy and X-Frame-Options headers to all responses as per OWASP secure design patterns.

test_cases.source_finding: Unprotected Storage of Credentials — Sensitive Data in Downloadable Files
test_cases.title: [A06] Unprotected Storage of Credentials — Sensitive Data in Downloadable Files — Download config.inc.php.dist and check for plaintext credentials
test_cases.steps: ["Send a GET request to /config/config.inc.php.dist.", "Review file contents for database credentials or secrets.", "Verify if credentials are stored in plaintext."]
test_cases.expected_result: Vulnerable: File contains plaintext credentials/secrets.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /config/config.inc.php.dist HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
test_cases.patched_response: Patched: File is not accessible or credentials are encrypted/removed.
test_cases.remediation: Store credentials securely and restrict access to configuration files as per OWASP secure storage guidance.

test_cases.source_finding: TLS 1.0/1.1 Enabled and Weak Cipher Suites
test_cases.title: [A04] TLS 1.0/1.1 Enabled and Weak Cipher Suites — Weak SSL/TLS Protocols Enabled (nuclei: weak-ssl-tls-protocols-enabled)
test_cases.steps: ["Run testssl against https://pentest-ground.com:4280/", "Review output for supported protocols: SSLv2, SSLv3, TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1", "Verify if any weak protocols are enabled"]
test_cases.expected_result: testssl output shows SSLv2, SSLv3, TLS 1.0, or TLS 1.1 as supported; weak CBC cipher suites (e.g., 3DES, RC4) are listed.
test_cases.http_request: N/A (testssl command: testssl https://pentest-ground.com:4280/)
test_cases.patched_response: testssl output shows only TLS 1.2 and TLS 1.3 enabled; no weak ciphers present.
test_cases.remediation: Disable SSLv2, SSLv3, TLS 1.0, and TLS 1.1 in Apache config. Only allow TLS 1.2+ with strong ciphers. Reference: OWASP A04 prevention guidance.

test_cases.source_finding: Missing HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) Header
test_cases.title: [A04] Missing HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) Header — Missing HSTS Header on Login Page
test_cases.steps: ["Send GET request to https://pentest-ground.com:4280/login.php using curl -v", "Check response headers for 'Strict-Transport-Security'", "Verify if HSTS is present and properly configured"]
test_cases.expected_result: No 'Strict-Transport-Security' header in response headers.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /login.php HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Connection: close
test_cases.patched_response: 'Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains' header present in response.
test_cases.remediation: Add 'Strict-Transport-Security' header to Apache config for all HTTPS responses. Reference: OWASP A04 prevention guidance.

test_cases.source_finding: Session Cookies Missing Secure and HttpOnly Flags
test_cases.title: [A04] Session Cookies Missing Secure and HttpOnly Flags — Session Cookie Missing Secure/HttpOnly Flags
test_cases.steps: ["Send GET request to https://pentest-ground.com:4280/login.php using curl -v", "Review 'Set-Cookie' headers for PHPSESSID and security cookies", "Check for 'Secure' and 'HttpOnly' flags"]
test_cases.expected_result: Set-Cookie header for PHPSESSID and security lacks 'Secure' and 'HttpOnly' attributes.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /login.php HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Connection: close
test_cases.patched_response: Set-Cookie header includes both 'Secure' and 'HttpOnly' flags for session cookies.
test_cases.remediation: Configure PHP and Apache to set 'Secure' and 'HttpOnly' flags on all session cookies. Reference: OWASP A04 prevention guidance.

test_cases.source_finding: Sensitive Data in URL Query String
test_cases.title: [A04] Sensitive Data in URL Query String — Credentials Transmitted in URL Query String
test_cases.steps: ["Send GET request to /vulnerabilities/brute/?username=admin&password=password&Login=Login", "Observe if credentials are visible in URL and logs", "Check if sensitive data is exposed in browser history or server logs"]
test_cases.expected_result: Credentials (username, password) are visible in URL, browser history, and potentially server logs.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /vulnerabilities/brute/?username=admin&password=password&Login=Login HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Connection: close
test_cases.patched_response: Credentials are transmitted via POST, not visible in URL; sensitive data never appears in query string.
test_cases.remediation: Change authentication endpoints to use POST for credential transmission. Never send sensitive data in URL parameters. Reference: OWASP A04 scenario guidance.

test_cases.title: [A04] Sensitive Data Caching in Browser — Sensitive Data Not Prevented from Browser Caching
test_cases.steps: ["Login to application and access /index.php", "Send GET request and review response headers for 'Cache-Control', 'Pragma', 'Expires'", "Check if sensitive data is allowed to be cached"]
test_cases.expected_result: Response lacks 'Cache-Control: no-cache, no-store, must-revalidate' and 'Pragma: no-cache'; sensitive data may be cached.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /index.php HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Cookie: PHPSESSID=<session_id>; security=low
Connection: close
test_cases.patched_response: Response includes 'Cache-Control: no-cache, no-store, must-revalidate' and 'Pragma: no-cache'; sensitive data is not cached.
test_cases.remediation: Configure application and web server to set 'Cache-Control: no-cache, no-store, must-revalidate' and 'Pragma: no-cache' for pages with sensitive data. Reference: OWASP A04 prevention guidance.

test_cases.title: [A04] Missing HTTPS Redirection (Downgrade Attack) — Downgrade Attack: HTTP Access Allowed to Login Page
test_cases.steps: ["Send GET request to http://pentest-ground.com:4280/login.php (note: HTTP, not HTTPS)", "Check if server redirects to HTTPS", "Verify if login page is accessible over HTTP"]
test_cases.expected_result: Login page is served over HTTP without redirect; credentials can be submitted over cleartext.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /login.php HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Connection: close
test_cases.patched_response: Server responds with 301/302 redirect to HTTPS for all HTTP requests.
test_cases.remediation: Configure Apache to redirect all HTTP requests to HTTPS. Reference: OWASP A04 prevention guidance.

test_cases.title: [A04] Mixed Content on Secure Pages — Mixed Content: Secure Page Loads Insecure Resources
test_cases.steps: ["Login and access https://pentest-ground.com:4280/index.php", "Review HTML source for resources loaded via http:// (scripts, images, stylesheets)", "Check browser console for mixed content warnings"]
test_cases.expected_result: Page loads resources (e.g., scripts, images) via http://, triggering browser mixed content warnings.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /index.php HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Cookie: PHPSESSID=<session_id>; security=low
Connection: close
test_cases.patched_response: All resources loaded via https://; no mixed content warnings.
test_cases.remediation: Update application to load all resources over HTTPS. Reference: OWASP A04 prevention guidance.

test_cases.title: [A04] Predictable Session IDs — Session ID Predictability Test
test_cases.steps: ["Send multiple login POST requests to /login.php", "Record issued PHPSESSID values", "Analyze session IDs for sequential or predictable patterns"]
test_cases.expected_result: PHPSESSID values are sequential or easily guessable (e.g., incrementing numbers).
test_cases.http_request:
POST /login.php HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Content-Length: <len>

username=admin&password=password&Login=Login&user_token=<token>
test_cases.patched_response: PHPSESSID values are random, high-entropy, and unpredictable.
test_cases.remediation: Configure PHP session management to use cryptographically secure random session IDs. Reference: OWASP A04 prevention guidance.

test_cases.title: [A04] Sensitive Information Disclosure via /config/ Directory — Sensitive Config Files Accessible (Key Management Error)
test_cases.steps: ["Send GET request to https://pentest-ground.com:4280/config/", "Review directory listing for files containing secrets (e.g., config.inc.php.dist)", "Download and inspect files for hard-coded keys or credentials"]
test_cases.expected_result: Config files containing database credentials or cryptographic keys are accessible and downloadable.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /config/ HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Connection: close
test_cases.patched_response: Config directory is not browseable; sensitive files are not accessible.
test_cases.remediation: Restrict access to config directories and files. Move secrets to secure storage (e.g., HSM, environment variables). Reference: OWASP A04 prevention guidance.

test_cases.title: [A02] Directory Listing Enabled on /config/ — Directory Listing Exposure — /config/ allows browsing (CWE-16)
test_cases.steps: ["Send a GET request to /config/", "Observe if the response contains a file index or listing of files", "Verify if sensitive files (e.g., config.inc.php.dist) are downloadable"]
test_cases.expected_result: Response contains an HTML directory index with links to files such as config.inc.php.dist. Files can be downloaded directly.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /config/ HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Connection: close
test_cases.patched_response: Response returns 403 Forbidden or a custom error page. No file listing or index is shown.
test_cases.remediation: Disable directory listing in the web server configuration (e.g., set 'Options -Indexes' in Apache). Remove or restrict access to sensitive directories. Follow a repeatable hardening process as per OWASP A02 prevention.

test_cases.title: [A02] Exposed phpinfo() Page — phpinfo() Disclosure — /phpinfo.php reveals environment (CWE-489)
test_cases.steps: ["Send a GET request to /phpinfo.php", "Review the response for PHP version, loaded modules, and configuration details", "Check for sensitive information such as enabled dangerous functions"]
test_cases.expected_result: Response contains the full phpinfo() output, including PHP version, modules, environment variables, and enabled functions.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /phpinfo.php HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Connection: close
test_cases.patched_response: Response returns 404 Not Found or 403 Forbidden. No phpinfo() output is visible.
test_cases.remediation: Remove phpinfo.php from production servers. Ensure no debug or sample files are deployed. Automate environment hardening and verify with each deployment.

test_cases.title: [A02] Missing Security Headers on /login.php — Missing Content-Security-Policy Header (CWE-16)
test_cases.steps: ["Send a GET request to /login.php", "Inspect the response headers for Content-Security-Policy", "Verify if the header is present and set to a restrictive value"]
test_cases.expected_result: No Content-Security-Policy header is present in the response headers.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /login.php HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Connection: close
test_cases.patched_response: Response includes a Content-Security-Policy header with a restrictive policy (e.g., default-src 'self').
test_cases.remediation: Add a strict Content-Security-Policy header to all responses. Automate header checks in deployment pipelines.

test_cases.title: [A02] Missing Security Headers on /login.php — Missing X-Frame-Options Header (CWE-16)
test_cases.steps: ["Send a GET request to /login.php", "Check the response headers for X-Frame-Options", "Verify if the header is present and set to DENY or SAMEORIGIN"]
test_cases.expected_result: No X-Frame-Options header is present in the response headers.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /login.php HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Connection: close
test_cases.patched_response: Response includes X-Frame-Options: DENY or X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN.
test_cases.remediation: Add X-Frame-Options: DENY or SAMEORIGIN to all responses to prevent clickjacking.

test_cases.title: [A02] Missing Security Headers on /login.php — Missing Strict-Transport-Security Header (CWE-16)
test_cases.steps: ["Send a GET request to /login.php over HTTPS", "Check the response headers for Strict-Transport-Security", "Verify if the header is present and set to a secure value"]
test_cases.expected_result: No Strict-Transport-Security header is present in the response headers.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /login.php HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Connection: close
test_cases.patched_response: Response includes Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload.
test_cases.remediation: Add Strict-Transport-Security header to enforce HTTPS and prevent downgrade attacks.

test_cases.title: [A02] Server Banner Disclosure on / — Server Header Leaks Apache Version (CWE-200)
test_cases.steps: ["Send a GET request to /", "Inspect the response headers for the Server header", "Check if the header reveals Apache version and OS"]
test_cases.expected_result: Server header discloses 'Apache/2.4.52 (Ubuntu)'.
test_cases.http_request:
GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Connection: close
test_cases.patched_response: Server header is absent or generic (e.g., 'Server: Apache'). No version or OS details are revealed.
test_cases.remediation: Configure the web server to suppress or obfuscate the Server header. Use 'ServerTokens Prod' and 'ServerSignature Off' in Apache.

test_cases.title: [A02] robots.txt Discloses Sensitive Paths — robots.txt Reveals Sensitive Directories (CWE-200)
test_cases.steps: ["Send a GET request to /robots.txt", "Review the response for Disallow directives", "Check if sensitive directories (e.g., /config/, /docs/, /vulnerabilities/) are listed"]
test_cases.expected_result: robots.txt contains Disallow entries for sensitive directories, revealing their existence.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /robots.txt HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Connection: close
test_cases.patched_response: robots.txt does not list sensitive directories or is absent. Only non-sensitive paths are listed.
test_cases.remediation: Avoid listing sensitive directories in robots.txt. Use proper access controls instead of relying on robots.txt for security.

test_cases.source_finding: Missing Secure and HttpOnly Flags on Session Cookies
test_cases.title: [A02] Missing Secure and HttpOnly Flags on Session Cookies — Session Cookies Lack Secure and HttpOnly Flags (CWE-315)
test_cases.steps: ["Send a GET request to /login.php", "Inspect the Set-Cookie headers in the response", "Check if session cookies (e.g., PHPSESSID, security) have Secure and HttpOnly flags"]
test_cases.expected_result: Set-Cookie headers for session cookies do not include Secure or HttpOnly attributes.
test_cases.http_request:
GET /login.php HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Connection: close
test_cases.patched_response: Set-Cookie headers include both Secure and HttpOnly flags for all session cookies.
test_cases.remediation: Configure the application to set Secure and HttpOnly flags on all session cookies. Automate checks for cookie attributes in deployment.

test_cases.title: [A02] Allowed HTTP Methods Not Restricted — OPTIONS Method Reveals Unnecessary HTTP Methods (CWE-16)
test_cases.steps: ["Send an OPTIONS request to /", "Check the Allow header in the response", "Verify if unnecessary methods (e.g., PUT, DELETE, TRACE) are enabled"]

test_cases.source_finding: OS Command Injection via ip parameter (CWE-78)
test_cases.title: [A05] OS Command Injection via ip parameter (CWE-78) — OS Command Injection using shell metacharacters
test_cases.steps: ["Log in as a valid user.", "Send a POST request to /vulnerabilities/exec/ with ip=127.0.0.1; id&Submit=Submit.", "Check if the response contains output from the 'id' command (e.g., 'uid=33(www-data)')."]
test_cases.expected_result: Response includes output of the 'id' command, such as 'uid=33(www-data)', confirming command execution.
test_cases.http_request:
POST /vulnerabilities/exec/ HTTP/1.1
Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded
Cookie: PHPSESSID=<valid-session>
Content-Length: 32

ip=127.0.0.1; id&Submit=Submit
test_cases.curl_command: curl.exe -sS -i -L --max-redirs 10 -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" -H "Cookie: PHPSESSID=$env:PHPSESSID; security=low" -H "Content-Length: 32" --data "ip=127.0.0.1; id&Submit=Submit" "https://pentest-ground.com:4280/vulnerabilities/exec/"
test_cases.patched_response: Response only includes expected ping output; no command output is present.
test_cases.remediation: Never pass user input directly to shell commands. Use safe APIs (e.g., escapeshellarg, escapeshellcmd) or avoid shell invocation entirely. Validate and sanitize all input.

test_cases.source_finding: Testing for CORS Misconfiguration (Wildcard Origin with Credentials)
test_cases.title: [A01] Testing for CORS Misconfiguration (Wildcard Origin with Credentials) — CORS: Origin Reflection with Credentials
test_cases.steps: ["Login as a regular user and obtain a valid session cookie.", "Send a GET request to /index.php with the header Origin: https://evil.com and the session cookie.", "Check if the response contains Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.com and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true."]
test_cases.expected_result: Response contains Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.com and Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, allowing cross-origin credentialed requests.

Host: pentest-ground.com:4280
Origin: https://evil.com
Cookie: PHPSESSID=<valid-session>
Connection: close
test_cases.curl_command: curl.exe -sS -i -L --max-redirs 10 -X GET -H "Origin: https://evil.com" -H "Cookie: PHPSESSID=$env:PHPSESSID; security=low" -H "Connection: close" "https://pentest-ground.com:4280/index.php"
test_cases.patched_response: Response does not reflect arbitrary origins or does not set Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true for untrusted origins.
test_cases.remediation: Restrict CORS to only allow trusted origins and never use Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true with a wildcard or reflected origin.

chain_attacks.attack_name: From Default Credentials to Full Server Compromise via OS Command Injection
chain_attacks.impact: An attacker can log in using default credentials, access authenticated functionality, and exploit OS command injection to execute arbitrary commands as www-data, leading to full server compromise.
chain_attacks.findings_used: ["Weak/Default Credentials \u2014 admin:password, others", "OS Command Injection \u2014 ip parameter shell-out"]

chain_attacks.attack_name: Unrestricted PHP File Upload Enables Persistent Remote Code Execution
chain_attacks.impact: An attacker can upload a malicious PHP webshell and execute arbitrary code on the server, maintaining persistent access.

chain_attacks.attack_name: Stored XSS in Guestbook Enables Session Hijacking Due to Insecure Cookies
chain_attacks.impact: An attacker can inject JavaScript into the guestbook, which executes for all users. Because session cookies lack Secure and HttpOnly flags, the script can steal session tokens, leading to account compromise.
chain_attacks.findings_used: ["Stored Cross-Site Scripting \u2014 guestbook", "Cookie Missing Secure and HttpOnly Flags"]

chain_attacks.attack_name: Reflected XSS Enables CSRF Token Theft and Account Takeover
chain_attacks.impact: An attacker can use reflected XSS to steal CSRF tokens or directly trigger state-changing actions, such as password changes, leading to account takeover.
chain_attacks.findings_used: ["Reflected Cross-Site Scripting \u2014 name parameter", "Cross-Site Request Forgery \u2014 password change"]

chain_attacks.attack_name: LFI Enables Disclosure of Sensitive Configuration Files via Directory Listing
chain_attacks.impact: An attacker can enumerate files via directory listing and use LFI to read sensitive files, such as configuration files containing database credentials.
chain_attacks.findings_used: ["Local File Inclusion \u2014 page parameter", "Directory Listing \u2014 /config/ browseable"]

chain_attacks.attack_name: phpinfo() Exposure Aids in Tailored Exploitation of File Upload and Command Injection
chain_attacks.impact: An attacker can use phpinfo() to discover enabled PHP functions and upload paths, making exploitation of file upload and command injection vulnerabilities more reliable.

chain_attacks.attack_name: robots.txt Enumeration Leads to Discovery and Exploitation of Sensitive Endpoints
chain_attacks.impact: An attacker can use robots.txt to find sensitive directories and endpoints, such as /config/ and /vulnerabilities/, increasing the likelihood of discovering and exploiting critical flaws.

chain_attacks.attack_name: Missing Security Headers Amplify XSS Impact
chain_attacks.impact: The absence of Content-Security-Policy and X-Frame-Options headers allows XSS payloads to execute without restriction and enables clickjacking attacks.

chain_attacks.attack_name: Outdated Backend Technology Increases Exploit Success Rate
chain_attacks.impact: Attackers can leverage public exploits for Apache 2.4.52 and PHP 7.4.33, as disclosed by server banners and phpinfo.php, to escalate privileges or bypass mitigations.
chain_attacks.findings_used: ["Server Banner Discloses Apache Version", "Backend Technology Disclosed \u2014 PHP/7.4.33 (EOL)"]

chain_attacks.attack_name: Weak TLS and Cookie Flags Enable Credential Theft via Network and Browser Attacks
chain_attacks.impact: Attackers can intercept credentials and session tokens due to weak TLS protocols and missing Secure/HttpOnly cookie flags, especially when combined with XSS.
chain_attacks.findings_used: ["TLS 1.0/1.1 Enabled + Weak CBC Ciphers", "Cookie Missing Secure and HttpOnly Flags"]

critical_advisories.title: Apache HTTP Server 2.4.52 — CVE-2021-44790

critical_advisories.title: Apache HTTP Server 2.4.52 — CVE-2023-25690
critical_advisories.compromise_narrative:
Some mod_proxy configurations on Apache HTTP Server versions 2.4.0 through 2.4.55 allow a HTTP Request Smuggling attack.




Configurations are affected when mod_proxy is enabled along with some form of RewriteRule
 or ProxyPassMatch in which a non-specific pattern matches
 some portion of the user-supplied request-target (URL) data and is then
 re-inserted into the proxied request-target using variable 
substitution. For example, something like:




RewriteEngine on
RewriteRule "^/here/(.*)" "http://example.com:8080/elsewhere?$1"; [P]
ProxyPassReverse /here/ http://example.com:8080/


Request splitting/smuggling could result in bypass of access controls in the proxy server, proxying unintended URLs to existing origin servers, and cache poisoning. Users are recommended to update to at least version 2.4.56 of Apache HTTP Server.
critical_advisories.impact: Pre-authentication exploitation of a third-party component running on the target. Public-internet attackers can attempt this without credentials.
critical_advisories.remediation: Upgrade Apache HTTP Server past the version range affected by CVE-2023-25690 and confirm the patched build via the Server header / banner. If immediate upgrade isn't possible, deploy compensating controls (WAF rule, network ACL) until patched.

critical_advisories.title: OpenSSL 1.1.1f — CVE-2021-3711
critical_advisories.compromise_narrative: In order to decrypt SM2 encrypted data an application is expected to call the API function EVP_PKEY_decrypt(). Typically an application will call this function twice. The first time, on entry, the "out" parameter can be NULL and, on exit, the "outlen" parameter is populated with the buffer size required to hold the decrypted plaintext. The application can then allocate a sufficiently sized buffer and call EVP_PKEY_decrypt() again, but this time passing a non-NULL value for the "out" parameter. A bug in the implementation of the SM2 decryption code means that the calculation of the buffer size required to hold the plaintext returned by the first call to EVP_PKEY_decrypt() can be smaller than the actual size required by the second call. This can lead to a buffer overflow when EVP_PKEY_decrypt() is called by the application a second time with a buffer that is too small. A malicious attacker who is able present SM2 content for decryption to an application could cause attacker chosen data to overflow the buffer by up to a maximum of 62 bytes altering the contents of other data held after the buffer, possibly changing application behaviour or causing the application to crash. The location of the buffer is application dependent but is typically heap allocated. Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1l (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1k).
critical_advisories.impact: Pre-authentication exploitation of a third-party component running on the target. Public-internet attackers can attempt this without credentials.
critical_advisories.remediation: Upgrade OpenSSL past the version range affected by CVE-2021-3711 and confirm the patched build via the Server header / banner. If immediate upgrade isn't possible, deploy compensating controls (WAF rule, network ACL) until patched.

critical_advisories.title: OpenSSL 1.1.1f — CVE-2026-28387
critical_advisories.compromise_narrative:
Issue summary: An uncommon configuration of clients performing DANE TLSA-based
server authentication, when paired with uncommon server DANE TLSA records, may
result in a use-after-free and/or double-free on the client side.

Impact summary: A use after free can have a range of potential consequences
such as the corruption of valid data, crashes or execution of arbitrary code.

However, the issue only affects clients that make use of TLSA records with both
the PKIX-TA(0/PKIX-EE(1) certificate usages and the DANE-TA(2) certificate
usage.

By far the most common deployment of DANE is in SMTP MTAs for which RFC7672
recommends that clients treat as 'unusable' any TLSA records that have the PKIX
certificate usages.  These SMTP (or other similar) clients are not vulnerable
to this issue.  Conversely, any clients that support only the PKIX usages, and
ignore the DANE-TA(2) usage are also not vulnerable.

The client would also need to be communicating with a server that publishes a
TLSA RRset with both types of TLSA records.

No FIPS modules are affected by this issue, the problem code is outside the
FIPS module boundary.
critical_advisories.impact: Pre-authentication exploitation of a third-party component running on the target. Public-internet attackers can attempt this without credentials.

critical_advisories.title: OpenSSL 1.1.1f — CVE-2023-4807
critical_advisories.compromise_narrative:
Issue summary: The POLY1305 MAC (message authentication code) implementation
contains a bug that might corrupt the internal state of applications on the
Windows 64 platform when running on newer X86_64 processors supporting the
AVX512-IFMA instructions.

Impact summary: If in an application that uses the OpenSSL library an attacker
can influence whether the POLY1305 MAC algorithm is used, the application
state might be corrupted with various application dependent consequences.

The POLY1305 MAC (message authentication code) implementation in OpenSSL does
not save the contents of non-volatile XMM registers on Windows 64 platform
when calculating the MAC of data larger than 64 bytes. Before returning to
the caller all the XMM registers are set to zero rather than restoring their
previous content. The vulnerable code is used only on newer x86_64 processors
supporting the AVX512-IFMA instructions.

The consequences of this kind of internal application state corruption can
be various - from no consequences, if the calling application does not
depend on the contents of non-volatile XMM registers at all, to the worst
consequences, where the attacker could get complete control of the application
process. However given the contents of the registers are just zeroized so
the attacker cannot put arbitrary values inside, the most likely consequence,
if any, would be an incorrect result of some application dependent
calculations or a crash leading to a denial of service.

The POLY1305 MAC algorithm is most frequently used as part of the
CHACHA20-POLY1305 AEAD (authenticated encryption with associated data)
algorithm. The most common usage of this AEAD cipher is with TLS protocol
versions 1.2 and 1.3 and a malicious client can influence whether this AEAD
cipher is used by the server. This implies that server applications using
OpenSSL can be potentially impacted. However we are currently not aware of
any concrete application that would be affected by this issue therefore we
consider this a Low severity security issue.

As a workaround the AVX512-IFMA instructions support can be disabled at
runtime by setting the environment variable OPENSSL_ia32cap:

   OPENSSL_ia32cap=:~0x200000

The FIPS provider is not affected by this issue.

critical_advisories.title: OpenSSL 1.1.1f — CVE-2020-1967
critical_advisories.compromise_narrative: Server or client applications that call the SSL_check_chain() function during or after a TLS 1.3 handshake may crash due to a NULL pointer dereference as a result of incorrect handling of the "signature_algorithms_cert" TLS extension. The crash occurs if an invalid or unrecognised signature algorithm is received from the peer. This could be exploited by a malicious peer in a Denial of Service attack. OpenSSL version 1.1.1d, 1.1.1e, and 1.1.1f are affected by this issue. This issue did not affect OpenSSL versions prior to 1.1.1d. Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1g (Affected 1.1.1d-1.1.1f).

critical_advisories.title: OpenSSL 1.1.1f — CVE-2021-23840
critical_advisories.compromise_narrative: Calls to EVP_CipherUpdate, EVP_EncryptUpdate and EVP_DecryptUpdate may overflow the output length argument in some cases where the input length is close to the maximum permissable length for an integer on the platform. In such cases the return value from the function call will be 1 (indicating success), but the output length value will be negative. This could cause applications to behave incorrectly or crash. OpenSSL versions 1.1.1i and below are affected by this issue. Users of these versions should upgrade to OpenSSL 1.1.1j. OpenSSL versions 1.0.2x and below are affected by this issue. However OpenSSL 1.0.2 is out of support and no longer receiving public updates. Premium support customers of OpenSSL 1.0.2 should upgrade to 1.0.2y. Other users should upgrade to 1.1.1j. Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1j (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1i). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2y (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2x).

critical_advisories.title: OpenSSL 1.1.1f — CVE-2022-0778
critical_advisories.compromise_narrative: The BN_mod_sqrt() function, which computes a modular square root, contains a bug that can cause it to loop forever for non-prime moduli. Internally this function is used when parsing certificates that contain elliptic curve public keys in compressed form or explicit elliptic curve parameters with a base point encoded in compressed form. It is possible to trigger the infinite loop by crafting a certificate that has invalid explicit curve parameters. Since certificate parsing happens prior to verification of the certificate signature, any process that parses an externally supplied certificate may thus be subject to a denial of service attack. The infinite loop can also be reached when parsing crafted private keys as they can contain explicit elliptic curve parameters. Thus vulnerable situations include: - TLS clients consuming server certificates - TLS servers consuming client certificates - Hosting providers taking certificates or private keys from customers - Certificate authorities parsing certification requests from subscribers - Anything else which parses ASN.1 elliptic curve parameters Also any other applications that use the BN_mod_sqrt() where the attacker can control the parameter values are vulnerable to this DoS issue. In the OpenSSL 1.0.2 version the public key is not parsed during initial parsing of the certificate which makes it slightly harder to trigger the infinite loop. However any operation which requires the public key from the certificate will trigger the infinite loop. In particular the attacker can use a self-signed certificate to trigger the loop during verification of the certificate signature. This issue affects OpenSSL versions 1.0.2, 1.1.1 and 3.0. It was addressed in the releases of 1.1.1n and 3.0.2 on the 15th March 2022. Fixed in OpenSSL 3.0.2 (Affected 3.0.0,3.0.1). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1n (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1m). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2zd (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2zc).

critical_advisories.title: OpenSSL 1.1.1f — CVE-2022-4450
critical_advisories.compromise_narrative:
The function PEM_read_bio_ex() reads a PEM file from a BIO and parses and
decodes the "name" (e.g. "CERTIFICATE"), any header data and the payload data.
If the function succeeds then the "name_out", "header" and "data" arguments are
populated with pointers to buffers containing the relevant decoded data. The
caller is responsible for freeing those buffers. It is possible to construct a
PEM file that results in 0 bytes of payload data. In this case PEM_read_bio_ex()
will return a failure code but will populate the header argument with a pointer
to a buffer that has already been freed. If the caller also frees this buffer
then a double free will occur. This will most likely lead to a crash. This
could be exploited by an attacker who has the ability to supply malicious PEM
files for parsing to achieve a denial of service attack.

The functions PEM_read_bio() and PEM_read() are simple wrappers around
PEM_read_bio_ex() and therefore these functions are also directly affected.

These functions are also called indirectly by a number of other OpenSSL
functions including PEM_X509_INFO_read_bio_ex() and
SSL_CTX_use_serverinfo_file() which are also vulnerable. Some OpenSSL internal
uses of these functions are not vulnerable because the caller does not free the
header argument if PEM_read_bio_ex() returns a failure code. These locations
include the PEM_read_bio_TYPE() functions as well as the decoders introduced in
OpenSSL 3.0.

The OpenSSL asn1parse command line application is also impacted by this issue.

critical_advisories.title: Unrestricted File Upload Enables Webshell Deployment and Remote Code Execution
critical_advisories.compromise_narrative: An attacker can upload a malicious PHP file to the /vulnerabilities/upload/ endpoint due to lack of extension and MIME validation. Once uploaded, the attacker can browse to the file and execute arbitrary PHP code, establishing a persistent webshell for full remote control of the server.
critical_advisories.impact: The attacker gains persistent remote code execution as the web server user, enabling full access to files, lateral movement, and potential privilege escalation.
critical_advisories.remediation: Restrict uploads to a server-side allow-list of safe extensions, validate MIME type using magic bytes, store uploads outside the web root, and serve files via a controller with Content-Disposition: attachment.
critical_advisories.exploit_steps: ["Login to the application and obtain a valid session cookie.", "Craft a multipart/form-data POST request to /vulnerabilities/upload/ with a file named shell.php containing '<?php echo shell_exec('id'); ?>'.", "After upload, access /hackable/uploads/shell.php in the browser or via curl to verify code execution."]

critical_advisories.title: OS Command Injection via ip Parameter Enables Remote Code Execution
critical_advisories.compromise_narrative: The ip parameter on /vulnerabilities/exec/ is unsafely passed to the shell, allowing attackers to inject arbitrary commands. This enables remote code execution as the web server user, which can be leveraged for further compromise.
critical_advisories.impact: The attacker can execute arbitrary system commands, potentially gaining access to sensitive data, pivoting to other systems, or escalating privileges.
critical_advisories.remediation: Sanitise all user input before passing to shell commands. Use parameterised functions and avoid direct shell invocation with user-controlled data.

critical_advisories.title: SQL Injection Enables Remote Code Execution via SELECT INTO OUTFILE
critical_advisories.compromise_narrative: The id parameter on /vulnerabilities/sqli/ is injectable, allowing attackers to execute SQL queries. In MySQL, this can be chained to SELECT INTO OUTFILE to write a PHP webshell into the web root, resulting in remote code execution.
critical_advisories.impact: The attacker can fully compromise the database and escalate to remote code execution, gaining persistent access to the server.
critical_advisories.remediation: Use parameterised queries for all database access. Remove FILE privilege from application DB users and set secure_file_priv to restrict file writes.

critical_advisories.title: Apache HTTP Server Path Traversal Enables Remote Code Execution (CVE-2021-42013)
critical_advisories.compromise_narrative: CVE-2021-42013 allows attackers to traverse directories and access files outside the intended web root. If CGI scripts are enabled or Alias-like directives are misconfigured, this can be exploited to execute arbitrary code on the server.
critical_advisories.impact: The attacker can gain remote code execution, potentially as root, leading to full server compromise.
critical_advisories.remediation: Apply the latest Apache HTTP Server patches. Ensure 'require all denied' is set for directories outside the web root and disable CGI scripts unless strictly necessary.

critical_advisories.title: Apache HTTP Server Path Traversal Enables Remote Code Execution (CVE-2021-41773)
critical_advisories.compromise_narrative: CVE-2021-41773 allows attackers to traverse directories and access files outside the intended web root. If CGI scripts are enabled or Alias-like directives are misconfigured, this can be exploited to execute arbitrary code on the server.
critical_advisories.impact: The attacker can gain remote code execution, potentially as root, leading to full server compromise.
critical_advisories.remediation: Apply the latest Apache HTTP Server patches. Ensure 'require all denied' is set for directories outside the web root and disable CGI scripts unless strictly necessary.

critical_advisories.title: From Default Credentials to Full Server Compromise via OS Command Injection
critical_advisories.compromise_narrative: An attacker can log in using default credentials, access authenticated functionality, and exploit OS command injection to execute arbitrary commands as www-data, leading to full server compromise.
critical_advisories.impact: The attacker gains full access to the server, including file system, database, and potential lateral movement.
critical_advisories.remediation: Enforce strong, unique credentials for all accounts. Remove default accounts and fix command injection vulnerabilities in authenticated endpoints.

critical_advisories.title: Unrestricted File Upload Enables Persistent Remote Code Execution
critical_advisories.compromise_narrative: An attacker can upload a malicious PHP webshell and execute arbitrary code on the server, maintaining persistent access.
critical_advisories.impact: The attacker maintains persistent remote code execution, enabling full control over the server and data.
critical_advisories.remediation: Restrict file uploads to safe extensions, validate MIME types, store files outside the web root, and serve via a controller.

The application can then allocate a sufficiently sized buffer and call EVP_PKEY_decrypt() again, but this time passing a non-NULL value for the "out" parameter. A bug in the implementation of the SM2 decryption code means that the calculation of the buffer size required to hold the plaintext returned by the first call to EVP_PKEY_decrypt() can be smaller than the actual size required by the second call. This can lead to a buffer overflow when EVP_PKEY_decrypt() is called by the application a second time with a buffer that is too small. A malicious attacker who is able present SM2 content for decryption to an application could cause attacker chosen data to overflow the buffer by up to a maximum of 62 bytes altering the contents of other data held after the buffer, possibly changing application behaviour or causing the application to crash. "cvss_v31": 8.1, "severity": "High", "summary": "Issue summary: An uncommon configuration of clients performing DANE TLSA-based
server authentication, when paired with uncommon server DANE TLSA records, may
result in a use-after-free and/or double-free on the client side.

Impact summary: A use after free can have a range of potential consequences
such as the corruption of valid data, crashes or execution of arbitrary code.

However, the issue only affects clients that make use of TLSA records with both
the PKIX-TA(0/PKIX-EE(1) certificate usages and the DANE-TA(2) certificate
usage.

By far the most common deployment of DANE is in SMTP MTAs for which RFC7672
recommends that clients treat as 'unusable' any TLSA records that have the PKIX
certificate usages.  These SMTP (or other similar) clients are not vulnerable
to this issue.  Conversely, any clients that support only the PKIX usages, and
ignore the DANE-TA(2) usage are also not vulnerable.

The client would also need to be communicating with a server that publishes a
TLSA RRset with both types of TLSA records.

No FIPS modules are affected by this issue, the problem code is outside the
FIPS module boundary.", "prerequisites": "", "exploit_steps": [], "references": ["https://github.com/openssl/openssl/commit/07e727d304746edb49a98ee8f6ab00256e1f012b", "https://github.com/openssl/openssl/commit/258a8f63b26995ba357f4326da00e19e29c6acbe", "https://github.com/openssl/openssl/commit/444958deaf450aea819171f97ae69eaedede42c3", "https://github.com/openssl/openssl/commit/7a4e08cee62a728d32e60b0de89e6764339df0a7", "https://github.com/openssl/openssl/commit/ec03fa050b3346997ed9c5fef3d0e16ad7db8177", "https://openssl-library.org/news/secadv/20260407.txt", "https://cert-portal.siemens.com/productcert/html/ssa-032379.html", "https://cert-portal.siemens.com/productcert/html/ssa-265688.html"], "in_kev": false, "ransomware_use": "Unknown", "source": "cve_kb"}, {"cve_id": "CVE-2023-4807", "cvss_v31": 7.8, "severity": "High", "summary": "Issue summary: The POLY1305 MAC (message authentication code) implementation
contains a bug that might corrupt the internal state of applications on the
Windows 64 platform when running on newer X86_64 processors supporting the
AVX512-IFMA instructions.

Impact summary: If in an application that uses the OpenSSL library an attacker
can influence whether the POLY1305 MAC algorithm is used, the application
state might be corrupted with various application dependent consequences.

The POLY1305 MAC (message authentication code) implementation in OpenSSL does
not save the contents of non-volatile XMM registers on Windows 64 platform
when calculating the MAC of data larger than 64 bytes. Before returning to
the caller all the XMM registers are set to zero rather than restoring their
previous content. The vulnerable code is used only on newer x86_64 processors
supporting the AVX512-IFMA instructions.

The consequences of this kind of internal application state corruption can
be various - from no consequences, if the calling application does not
depend on the contents of non-volatile XMM registers at all, to the worst
consequences, where the attacker could get complete control of the application
process. However given the contents of the registers are just zeroized so
the attacker cannot put arbitrary values inside, the most likely consequence,
if any, would be an incorrect result of some application dependent
calculations or a crash leading to a denial of service.

The POLY1305 MAC algorithm is most frequently used as part of the
CHACHA20-POLY1305 AEAD (authenticated encryption with associated data)
algorithm. The most common usage of this AEAD cipher is with TLS protocol
versions 1.2 and 1.3 and a malicious client can influence whether this AEAD
cipher is used by the server. This implies that server applications using
OpenSSL can be potentially impacted. However we are currently not aware of
any concrete application that would be affected by this issue therefore we
consider this a Low severity security issue.

As a workaround the AVX512-IFMA instructions support can be disabled at
runtime by setting the environment variable OPENSSL_ia32cap:

   OPENSSL_ia32cap=:~0x200000

The FIPS provider is not affected by this issue.", "prerequisites": "", "exploit_steps": [], "references": ["https://git.openssl.org/gitweb/?p=openssl.git;a=commitdiff;h=4bfac4471f53c4f74c8d81020beb938f92d84ca5", "https://git.openssl.org/gitweb/?p=openssl.git;a=commitdiff;h=6754de4a121ec7f261b16723180df6592cbb4508", "https://git.openssl.org/gitweb/?p=openssl.git;a=commitdiff;h=a632d534c73eeb3e3db8c7540d811194ef7c79ff", "https://www.openssl.org/news/secadv/20230908.txt", "https://git.openssl.org/gitweb/?p=openssl.git;a=commitdiff;h=4bfac4471f53c4f74c8d81020beb938f92d84ca5", "https://git.openssl.org/gitweb/?p=openssl.git;a=commitdiff;h=6754de4a121ec7f261b16723180df6592cbb4508", "https://git.openssl.org/gitweb/?p=openssl.git;a=commitdiff;h=a632d534c73eeb3e3db8c7540d811194ef7c79ff", "https://security.netapp.com/advisory/ntap-20230921-0001/", "https://www.openssl.org/news/secadv/20230908.txt"], "in_kev": false, "ransomware_use": "Unknown", "source": "cve_kb"}, {"cve_id": "CVE-2020-1967", "cvss_v31": 7.5, "severity": "High", "summary": "Server or client applications that call the SSL_check_chain() function during or after a TLS 1.3 handshake may crash due to a NULL pointer dereference as a result of incorrect handling of the "signature_algorithms_cert" TLS extension. forever for non-prime moduli. Internally this function is used when parsing certificates that contain elliptic curve public keys in compressed form or explicit elliptic curve parameters with a base point encoded in compressed form. It is possible to trigger the infinite loop by crafting a certificate that has invalid explicit curve parameters. Since certificate parsing happens prior to verification of the certificate signature, any process that parses an externally supplied certificate may thus be subject to a denial of service attack. The infinite loop can also be reached when parsing crafted private keys as they can contain explicit elliptic curve parameters. Thus vulnerable situations include: - TLS clients consuming server certificates - TLS servers consuming client certificates - Hosting providers taking certificates or private keys from customers - Certificate authorities parsing certification requests from subscribers - Anything else which parses ASN.1 elliptic curve parameters Also any other applications that use the BN_mod_sqrt() where the attacker can control the parameter values are vulnerable to this DoS issue. In the OpenSSL 1.0.2 version the public key is not parsed during initial parsing of the certificate which makes it slightly harder to trigger the infinite loop. However any operation which requires the public key from the certificate will trigger the infinite loop. In particular the attacker can use a self-signed certificate to trigger the loop during verification of the certificate signature. This issue affects OpenSSL versions 1.0.2, 1.1.1 and 3.0. It was addressed in the releases of 1.1.1n and 3.0.2 on the 15th March 2022. Fixed in OpenSSL 3.0.2 (Affected 3.0.0,3.0.1). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1n (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1m). "cve_kb"}, {"cve_id": "CVE-2022-4450", "cvss_v31": 7.5, "severity": "High", "summary": "The function PEM_read_bio_ex() reads a PEM file from a BIO and parses and
decodes the "name" (e.g. "CERTIFICATE"), any header data and the payload data.
If the function succeeds then the "name_out", "header" and "data" arguments are
populated with pointers to buffers containing the relevant decoded data. The
caller is responsible for freeing those buffers. It is possible to construct a
PEM file that results in 0 bytes of payload data. In this case PEM_read_bio_ex()
will return a failure code but will populate the header argument with a pointer
to a buffer that has already been freed. If the caller also frees this buffer
then a double free will occur. This will most likely lead to a crash. This
could be exploited by an attacker who has the ability to supply malicious PEM
files for parsing to achieve a denial of service attack.

The functions PEM_read_bio() and PEM_read() are simple wrappers around
PEM_read_bio_ex() and therefore these functions are also directly affected.

These functions are also called indirectly by a number of other OpenSSL
functions including PEM_X509_INFO_read_bio_ex() and
SSL_CTX_use_serverinfo_file() which are also vulnerable. Some OpenSSL internal
uses of these functions are not vulnerable because the caller does not free the
header argument if PEM_read_bio_ex() returns a failure code. These locations
include the PEM_read_bio_TYPE() functions as well as the decoders introduced in
OpenSSL 3.0.

ASN.1 data via a BIO. It is primarily used internally to OpenSSL to support the
SMIME, CMS and PKCS7 streaming capabilities, but may also be called directly by
end user applications.

The function receives a BIO from the caller, prepends a new BIO_f_asn1 filter
BIO onto the front of it to form a BIO chain, and then returns the new head of
the BIO chain to the caller. Under certain conditions, for example if a CMS
recipient public key is invalid, the new filter BIO is freed and the function
returns a NULL result indicating a failure. However, in this case, the BIO chain
is not properly cleaned up and the BIO passed by the caller still retains
internal pointers to the previously freed filter BIO. If the caller then goes on
to call BIO_pop() on the BIO then a use-after-free will occur. This will most
likely result in a crash.



This scenario occurs directly in the internal function B64_write_ASN1() which
may cause BIO_new_NDEF() to be called and will subsequently call BIO_pop() on
the BIO. This internal function is in turn called by the public API functions
PEM_write_bio_ASN1_stream, PEM_write_bio_CMS_stream, PEM_write_bio_PKCS7_stream,
SMIME_write_ASN1, SMIME_write_CMS and SMIME_write_PKCS7.

Other public API functions that may be impacted by this include
i2d_ASN1_bio_stream, BIO_new_CMS, BIO_new_PKCS7, i2d_CMS_bio_stream and
i2d_PKCS7_bio_stream.

validating the type, causing an invalid or NULL pointer dereference when

malformed TimeStamp Response can be caused to dereference an invalid or
NULL pointer when reading, resulting in a Denial of Service.

access the signing cert attribute value without validating its type.

TimeStamp Response to an application that verifies timestamp responses. The
TimeStamp protocol (RFC 3161) is not widely used and the impact of the
exploit is just a Denial of Service. For these reasons the issue was
assessed as Low severity.

as the TimeStamp Response implementation is outside the OpenSSL FIPS module
boundary.

OpenSSL 3.6, 3.5, 3.4, 3.3, 3.0 and 1.1.1 are vulnerable to this issue.
